section: Psychological, Social, and Biological Foundations of Behavior
provenance: Original content. Concepts calibrated against MCAT QBook Ch3 (Psychology) + Ch6 (Sociology) used privately as ground truth; no source text reproduced.
trademark_notice: MCAT is a registered trademark of the AAMC. This material is independently authored and not endorsed by the AAMC.

passages.title: Competing accounts of personality in a longitudinal study
passages.passage_text: A research team follows a cohort of volunteers for two decades, administering repeated self-report questionnaires and gathering peer ratings. The investigators want to interpret their data through several classic frameworks of personality: a psychoanalytic account emphasizing unconscious conflict, a humanistic account emphasizing growth toward one's potential, a trait model that scores each participant on five broad dimensions, and a social-cognitive account emphasizing the interplay of person and situation. One participant, an aspiring musician, is described as highly organized, dependable, and disciplined but also prone to worry and self-doubt.

questions.stem: In the Big Five (five-factor) model, the participant's tendency to be organized, dependable, and disciplined is best captured by which dimension?
questions.explanation: Conscientiousness is the Big Five factor describing organization, dependability, self-discipline, and goal-directed diligence. The participant's careful, disciplined style maps directly onto it.

distractor_notes.B: Openness reflects imagination, curiosity, and receptiveness to new experience, not orderliness.
distractor_notes.C: Agreeableness reflects warmth, cooperation, and trust toward others, not personal organization.
distractor_notes.D: Extraversion reflects sociability and positive emotionality, not disciplined self-regulation.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Big Five dimensions (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The participant's tendency toward worry and self-doubt is most closely associated with a high standing on which Big Five dimension?
questions.explanation: Neuroticism describes the tendency to experience negative emotions such as anxiety, worry, and emotional instability. Chronic self-doubt reflects a high neuroticism score.

distractor_notes.A: Agreeableness concerns interpersonal warmth and cooperation, not emotional volatility.
distractor_notes.C: Openness concerns curiosity and aesthetic sensitivity, not proneness to worry.
distractor_notes.D: Conscientiousness concerns discipline and orderliness, a separate dimension from emotional negativity.

questions.ground_truth_ref: neuroticism dimension (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: According to the psychoanalytic framework the team considers, the component of personality that operates on the reality principle and mediates between instinctual urges and moral constraints is the:
questions.explanation: In Freud's model, the ego operates on the reality principle and negotiates between the id's demands for immediate gratification and the superego's moral standards, finding realistic ways to satisfy urges.

distractor_notes.A: The id operates on the pleasure principle and seeks immediate gratification, not mediation.
distractor_notes.B: The superego embodies internalized moral standards; it constrains rather than mediates.
distractor_notes.D: The collective unconscious is Jung's concept, not a mediating structure in Freud's tripartite model.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Freudian id/ego/superego (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The humanistic account the team applies is best exemplified by which idea?

options.A: People are motivated by an innate drive toward self-actualization and personal growth
options.B: Behavior is shaped by schedules of reinforcement
options.C: Personality reflects stable, heritable trait dimensions
options.D: Adult conflicts stem from unresolved psychosexual stages

questions.explanation: Humanistic theories (e.g., Rogers, Maslow) emphasize an inherent tendency toward self-actualization: people are motivated to grow, fulfill their potential, and achieve congruence between the self and experience.

distractor_notes.C: Heritable trait dimensions describe trait/biological theories, not humanism.
distractor_notes.D: Unresolved psychosexual stages describe the psychoanalytic view, not the humanistic one.
distractor_notes.B: Reinforcement schedules belong to behaviorism, not the humanistic perspective.

questions.ground_truth_ref: humanistic self-actualization (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: A psychiatric clinic and models of mental disorder
passages.passage_text: A clinic evaluates several patients. One reports recurrent, unexpected surges of intense fear accompanied by a pounding heart and a sense of impending doom, with no obvious trigger. A second has felt persistently sad and lost interest in nearly all activities for over a month, with disrupted sleep and appetite. A third experiences delusions and disorganized speech alongside a flat affect. Clinicians debate whether to frame these conditions strictly in terms of brain chemistry or to also weigh psychological and social contributors.

questions.stem: The first patient's recurrent, unexpected surges of intense fear with physical symptoms and no clear trigger are most characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Panic disorder is defined by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks: abrupt surges of intense fear with somatic symptoms (racing heart, shortness of breath, a sense of impending doom) that occur without an identifiable external trigger.

distractor_notes.A: A specific phobia produces fear in response to a particular identifiable object or situation, not unexpected attacks.
distractor_notes.C: OCD is defined by intrusive obsessions and compulsive rituals, not spontaneous panic attacks.
distractor_notes.D: Major depressive disorder centers on persistent low mood and anhedonia, not sudden fear surges.

questions.ground_truth_ref: panic disorder features (QBook Ch3) - original vignette

questions.stem: The second patient's month of persistent sadness, loss of interest, and disturbed sleep and appetite most clearly fits which diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Major depressive disorder requires a period of depressed mood or loss of interest (anhedonia) lasting at least two weeks, accompanied by symptoms such as changes in sleep, appetite, and energy. The vignette matches this profile.

distractor_notes.A: Bipolar I requires at least one manic episode; no mania is described here.
distractor_notes.B: Generalized anxiety disorder features chronic, excessive worry rather than pervasive sadness and anhedonia.
distractor_notes.D: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms concurrent with mood episodes, which are not described.

questions.ground_truth_ref: major depressive disorder criteria (QBook Ch3) - original vignette

questions.stem: The third patient's delusions, disorganized speech, and flat affect illustrate that schizophrenia involves both positive and negative symptoms. Which pairing is correct?

options.A: Delusions are a negative symptom; flat affect is a positive symptom
options.B: Both delusions and flat affect are negative symptoms
options.C: Both delusions and flat affect are positive symptoms
options.D: Delusions are a positive symptom; flat affect is a negative symptom

questions.explanation: Positive symptoms are additions to normal experience (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech). Negative symptoms are losses or reductions of normal function (flat affect, avolition, alogia). Thus delusions are positive and flat affect is negative.

distractor_notes.A: This reverses the definitions; delusions are additions (positive) and flat affect is a deficit (negative).
distractor_notes.C: Flat affect is a reduction in normal emotional expression, a negative symptom, not a positive one.
distractor_notes.B: Delusions are an added abnormal experience, a positive symptom, not a negative one.

questions.ground_truth_ref: positive vs negative symptoms of schizophrenia (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia is supported by the observation that many antipsychotic drugs:

options.A: Increase dopamine release, reducing positive symptoms
options.D: Block dopamine receptors, reducing positive symptoms

questions.explanation: The dopamine hypothesis links positive symptoms to excess dopaminergic activity. Classic antipsychotics act as dopamine (D2) receptor antagonists, and their ability to reduce positive symptoms supports the hypothesis.

distractor_notes.A: Increasing dopamine would be expected to worsen, not reduce, positive symptoms.
distractor_notes.B: Blocking serotonin reuptake describes SSRIs used for depression, not the core action supporting the dopamine hypothesis.
distractor_notes.C: Enhancing GABA describes anxiolytics/sedatives, not the mechanism central to the dopamine hypothesis.

questions.ground_truth_ref: dopamine hypothesis / antipsychotics (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: Clinicians who insist on framing these disorders strictly in terms of brain chemistry are adopting a biomedical model. Compared with this, the biopsychosocial model additionally emphasizes:

options.A: Psychological and social factors alongside biological ones
options.D: That mental disorders are not real illnesses

questions.explanation: The biopsychosocial model holds that health and illness result from the interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors. It broadens the strictly biological (biomedical) view by adding psychological and social contributors.

distractor_notes.B: Restricting causes to genes and neurochemistry is the biomedical model, the very view being expanded upon.
distractor_notes.C: Supernatural explanations are pre-scientific and not part of the biopsychosocial framework.
distractor_notes.D: The biopsychosocial model treats disorders as real; it does not deny their reality.

questions.ground_truth_ref: biomedical vs biopsychosocial model (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: Tracking children through developmental milestones
passages.passage_text: A developmental laboratory studies children from infancy through adolescence. Investigators note when infants first search for a hidden toy, when preschoolers can and cannot take another person's visual perspective, and how children of different ages justify moral choices in dilemmas. They also assess infants' reactions when a caregiver briefly leaves and then returns during a standardized separation procedure.

questions.stem: An infant who searches for a toy after it is hidden under a blanket has most likely acquired:
questions.explanation: Object permanence, acquired during Piaget's sensorimotor stage, is the understanding that objects continue to exist even when out of sight. Searching for a hidden toy demonstrates it.

distractor_notes.A: Conservation is the understanding that quantity is unchanged despite changes in appearance, emerging in the concrete operational stage.
distractor_notes.C: Abstract reasoning develops in the formal operational stage, much later than infancy.
distractor_notes.D: Theory of mind concerns understanding others' mental states, not the persistence of objects.

questions.ground_truth_ref: object permanence / sensorimotor stage (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: A preschooler who cannot take another person's visual perspective and assumes others see what she sees is displaying a hallmark of Piaget's:
questions.explanation: Egocentrism, the inability to distinguish one's own perspective from another's, is characteristic of the preoperational stage (roughly ages 2-7).

distractor_notes.A: The sensorimotor stage (birth to ~2) is defined by sensory-motor exploration and object permanence, not egocentric perspective-taking failures in speech.
distractor_notes.B: In the concrete operational stage children overcome egocentrism and can take others' perspectives on concrete matters.
distractor_notes.D: The formal operational stage is marked by abstract, hypothetical reasoning, well beyond egocentrism.

questions.ground_truth_ref: preoperational egocentrism (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: In Kohlberg's theory, a child who says stealing is wrong only because 'you might get caught and punished' is reasoning at which level?
questions.explanation: Preconventional morality bases judgments on direct consequences to the self, such as avoiding punishment or gaining reward. Focusing on getting caught reflects this earliest level.

distractor_notes.A: Conventional morality emphasizes social rules and gaining approval, not simple avoidance of punishment.
distractor_notes.B: Postconventional morality appeals to abstract ethical principles and universal rights.
distractor_notes.C: 'Transconventional' is not a level in Kohlberg's model.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Kohlberg preconventional level (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: In the standardized separation procedure, an infant who explores when the caregiver is present, is distressed at departure, and is readily comforted at reunion is best classified as:
questions.explanation: In Ainsworth's Strange Situation, secure infants use the caregiver as a base for exploration, show distress on separation, and are quickly soothed on reunion. That pattern is described here.

distractor_notes.B: Anxious-ambivalent infants are intensely distressed and remain hard to comfort, often resisting contact at reunion.
distractor_notes.C: Avoidant infants show little distress at separation and ignore or avoid the caregiver at reunion.
distractor_notes.D: Disorganized attachment shows inconsistent, contradictory behaviors without a coherent strategy.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Ainsworth Strange Situation / secure attachment (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: According to Erikson, an adolescent's central psychosocial task is to resolve the conflict of:
questions.explanation: In Erikson's stages, adolescence centers on identity vs. role confusion: developing a coherent sense of self and personal identity. Failure yields confusion about one's role and values.

distractor_notes.A: Trust vs. mistrust is the infancy stage.
distractor_notes.C: Generativity vs. stagnation is the middle-adulthood stage.
distractor_notes.D: Integrity vs. despair is the late-adulthood stage.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Erikson identity vs role confusion (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: A sleep laboratory studies consciousness and drugs
passages.passage_text: Technicians in a sleep laboratory record electroencephalography (EEG) as volunteers cycle through the night. They observe distinct stages, including one in which the EEG resembles wakefulness even though the skeletal muscles are nearly paralyzed and vivid dreaming occurs. Some volunteers are given various psychoactive substances so the team can compare their effects on arousal and behavior. The team also examines how the body's roughly 24-hour internal clock is entrained to the light-dark cycle.

questions.stem: The sleep stage in which EEG resembles wakefulness, skeletal muscles are nearly paralyzed, and vivid dreaming occurs is:
questions.explanation: REM (rapid eye movement) sleep shows a low-voltage, fast EEG resembling waking, accompanied by muscle atonia (near-paralysis) and vivid dreams.

distractor_notes.A: Stage 1 is a light transitional stage with theta waves, not the wake-like EEG with atonia and vivid dreaming.
distractor_notes.B: Slow-wave sleep is dominated by high-amplitude delta waves, the opposite of a wake-like EEG.
distractor_notes.D: The hypnagogic state is the drowsy transition into sleep, not a defined stage with muscle atonia and vivid dreaming.

questions.ground_truth_ref: REM sleep features (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: Which EEG pattern is most characteristic of slow-wave (stage 3) sleep?

options.C: Sleep spindles as the dominant feature

questions.explanation: Slow-wave sleep is defined by high-amplitude, low-frequency delta waves, the deepest stage of non-REM sleep.

distractor_notes.A: Beta waves reflect alert, active wakefulness, not deep sleep.
distractor_notes.B: Alpha waves appear during relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed, before sleep onset.
distractor_notes.C: Sleep spindles and K-complexes are hallmarks of stage 2, not the delta-dominated stage 3.

questions.ground_truth_ref: delta waves in slow-wave sleep (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The roughly 24-hour internal clock that is entrained by light is regulated primarily by the:
questions.explanation: The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus is the master circadian pacemaker; it receives light input from the retina and coordinates the ~24-hour rhythm, including melatonin release from the pineal gland.

distractor_notes.B: The medulla governs autonomic functions like breathing and heart rate, not the circadian clock.
distractor_notes.C: The cerebellum coordinates movement and balance, not circadian timing.
distractor_notes.D: The hippocampus is central to memory formation, not circadian regulation.

questions.ground_truth_ref: SCN circadian pacemaker (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: One volunteer is given a substance that increases alertness, heart rate, and blood pressure and elevates synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine. This substance is best classified as a:
questions.explanation: Stimulants (e.g., cocaine, amphetamines) increase arousal and sympathetic activity and raise synaptic monoamines such as dopamine and norepinephrine, heightening alertness, heart rate, and blood pressure.

distractor_notes.A: Depressants (e.g., alcohol, benzodiazepines) reduce CNS arousal, lowering heart rate and alertness.
distractor_notes.C: Opioids act on opioid receptors to relieve pain and produce sedation and euphoria, not the described sympathetic activation.
distractor_notes.D: Hallucinogens primarily distort perception; they are not defined by the monoamine-driven arousal described.

questions.ground_truth_ref: stimulant class effects (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: Attitudes and attitude change at a public forum
passages.passage_text: Researchers survey attendees before and after a public forum on a local policy. Some attendees carefully scrutinize the strength of the arguments presented, while others are swayed mainly by the speaker's confident tone and attractiveness. A few attendees who had freely volunteered to argue the opposite of their private view later report that their private attitudes shifted toward the position they had argued. The team analyzes how attitudes form, change, and relate to behavior.

questions.stem: Attendees who are persuaded by carefully scrutinizing the strength and logic of the arguments are using which route of the elaboration likelihood model?
questions.explanation: In the elaboration likelihood model, the central route involves effortful, careful evaluation of the content and quality of arguments. Attitude change through this route tends to be more durable.

distractor_notes.A: The peripheral route relies on superficial cues (e.g., speaker attractiveness) rather than argument quality.
distractor_notes.B: 'Heuristic route' is a term from a related model; it is not the ELM route described by careful argument scrutiny.
distractor_notes.D: 'Affective route' is not one of the two routes named in the elaboration likelihood model.

questions.ground_truth_ref: elaboration likelihood model central route (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: Attendees swayed mainly by the speaker's confident tone and attractiveness rather than argument quality are being persuaded via the:

options.B: Cognitive route, based on argument logic
options.D: Peripheral route, relying on superficial cues

questions.explanation: The peripheral route to persuasion relies on incidental cues such as a speaker's attractiveness or confidence rather than the substance of the message. Attitude change via this route is typically weaker and less durable.

distractor_notes.A: The central route depends on argument quality, not surface cues.
distractor_notes.B: Judging argument logic is the central route; 'cognitive route' mislabels the described process.
distractor_notes.C: Foot-in-the-door is a compliance technique, not a persuasion route in the ELM.

questions.ground_truth_ref: elaboration likelihood model peripheral route (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The attendees who argued against their private view and then shifted their private attitudes best illustrate:
questions.explanation: Freely advocating a counterattitudinal position with insufficient external justification creates cognitive dissonance. To reduce the discomfort, people often bring their private attitudes into line with the behavior they performed.

distractor_notes.B: The mere exposure effect is increased liking from repeated exposure, not attitude change from counterattitudinal action.
distractor_notes.C: The bystander effect concerns reduced helping in groups, unrelated to attitude change.
distractor_notes.D: Deindividuation is loss of self-awareness in groups, not the mechanism here.

questions.ground_truth_ref: cognitive dissonance (QBook Ch3) - original vignette

questions.stem: One participant reports that his memory of a past bad experience with the policy shapes his opposition. This memory represents which component of the tripartite (ABC) model of attitudes?
questions.explanation: The ABC model divides attitudes into affective (feelings/emotional reactions), behavioral (actions/tendencies), and cognitive (beliefs/thoughts) components. An emotionally charged memory driving opposition is affective.

distractor_notes.A: The cognitive component refers to beliefs and thoughts about the object, not the emotional reaction itself.
distractor_notes.C: The behavioral component refers to how the attitude is expressed in action, not a felt emotional memory.
distractor_notes.D: 'Conative' is not part of the standard affective-behavioral-cognitive model as used here.

questions.ground_truth_ref: ABC components of attitudes (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: Attribution, conformity, and group influence
passages.passage_text: A social psychology course reviews classic findings. In one study, participants publicly agree with an obviously incorrect group judgment about line lengths. In another, participants comply with an authority figure's instructions to a surprising degree. Students also discuss how observers explain others' behavior versus their own, and how the presence of others changes individual effort and performance on tasks.

questions.stem: Participants who publicly agree with an obviously wrong group judgment about line lengths are demonstrating:
questions.explanation: Adjusting one's expressed judgment to match a group, even against clear perceptual evidence, is conformity (as in Asch's line studies), driven by normative social pressure.

distractor_notes.A: Obedience involves complying with a direct order from an authority figure, not matching peers' judgments.
distractor_notes.B: The fundamental attribution error concerns explaining behavior, not yielding to group judgments.
distractor_notes.D: Social loafing is reduced individual effort in a group task, not conforming to a group's opinion.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Asch conformity (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: Participants who follow an authority figure's instructions to a surprising degree, even when uncomfortable, best illustrate:
questions.explanation: Obedience is behavior change in response to a direct command from a perceived authority, as in Milgram's studies. The scenario describes following an authority's instructions despite discomfort.

distractor_notes.A: Conformity is yielding to peer group norms, not to a specific authority's orders.
distractor_notes.B: Group polarization is the strengthening of group members' average view after discussion, not obedience.
distractor_notes.C: Reciprocity-based compliance involves returning a favor, not following authority commands.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Milgram obedience (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: An observer sees a stranger trip and immediately concludes the stranger is clumsy, ignoring the icy sidewalk. This overemphasis on disposition over situation when judging others is:

options.D: The actor-observer effect applied to the self

questions.explanation: The fundamental attribution error is the tendency to attribute others' behavior to internal dispositions while underweighting situational causes. Judging the person clumsy rather than blaming the ice is a classic example.

distractor_notes.B: The self-serving bias explains attributions about one's own successes and failures, not judgments of others.
distractor_notes.C: The just-world hypothesis is the belief that people get what they deserve, a distinct bias.
distractor_notes.D: The actor-observer effect notes that we attribute our own behavior to situations; applying it to the self does not describe judging a stranger dispositionally.

questions.ground_truth_ref: fundamental attribution error (QBook Ch3) - original vignette

questions.stem: After a group discussion, members who initially leaned mildly toward a decision end up endorsing a more extreme version of it. This shift is best described as:
questions.explanation: Group polarization is the tendency for group discussion to strengthen the members' initial leanings, producing a more extreme average position than individuals held beforehand.

distractor_notes.A: Groupthink is the drive for consensus that suppresses dissent and degrades decision quality, not simply intensification of a leaning.
distractor_notes.C: Social facilitation is improved performance on simple tasks when others are present, unrelated to attitude extremity.
distractor_notes.D: Bystander apathy is reduced helping when others are present, not an attitude shift.

questions.ground_truth_ref: group polarization (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: Wealth, class, and social mobility across generations
passages.passage_text: A sociology team studies inequality in a large nation. They document how income and accumulated assets differ across households, how children's eventual class positions relate to those of their parents, and how some societies allow movement between strata while others fix a person's position at birth. The team contrasts theoretical accounts of what defines a person's place in the stratification system.

questions.stem: A society in which a person's social position is fixed at birth and movement between strata is prohibited is best described as a:
questions.explanation: A caste system assigns social position at birth and prohibits movement between strata, with status ascribed rather than achieved.

distractor_notes.A: A class system permits at least some social mobility based on achieved factors.
distractor_notes.D: 'Estate of upward mobility' is not a recognized stratification type and contradicts the fixed-at-birth description.
distractor_notes.B: A meritocracy allocates position based on ability and effort, the opposite of birth-fixed status.

questions.ground_truth_ref: caste vs class system (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: A factory worker who is laid off and later becomes a lower-paid store clerk within his own lifetime has experienced:
questions.explanation: Intragenerational mobility refers to changes in an individual's social position within his or her own lifetime. Moving to a lower-paying position is downward mobility, so this is intragenerational downward mobility.

distractor_notes.A: Intergenerational mobility compares a person's position with that of their parents, not within one lifetime.
distractor_notes.C: The move described is downward and within one lifetime, not intergenerational upward.
distractor_notes.B: Structural mobility refers to shifts driven by economy-wide changes affecting many people, not this individual's job change.

questions.ground_truth_ref: intragenerational mobility (QBook Ch6) - original vignette

questions.stem: In distinguishing wealth from income, which best describes wealth?

options.A: The total value of accumulated assets minus debts
options.B: The salary and wages a household earns each year
options.C: A measure identical to annual income
options.D: The prestige attached to a person's occupation

questions.explanation: Wealth is the net value of accumulated assets (property, investments, savings) minus liabilities. Income is the flow of earnings over a period, a distinct concept.

distractor_notes.B: Salary and wages are income, the flow of earnings, not accumulated wealth.
distractor_notes.C: Wealth and income are not identical; wealth is a stock of assets, income is a flow.
distractor_notes.D: Occupational prestige is a status dimension, not a definition of wealth.

questions.ground_truth_ref: wealth vs income (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: The team notes that Max Weber, unlike Karl Marx, argued that stratification is multidimensional. Weber held that social standing is determined by:

options.A: Only one's relationship to the means of production
options.C: Solely the amount of accumulated wealth

questions.explanation: Weber proposed a multidimensional view of stratification based on class (economic position), status (social prestige/honor), and party (political power), broadening Marx's focus on economic class alone.

distractor_notes.A: Relationship to the means of production is Marx's single economic axis, the view Weber expanded.
distractor_notes.C: Weber did not reduce standing to wealth alone; he added prestige and power.
distractor_notes.D: Prestige is only one of Weber's three dimensions, not the whole account.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Weber vs Marx stratification (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: When wealthy parents pass advantages to children who then remain wealthy, keeping resources within families across generations, sociologists call this:
questions.explanation: Social reproduction is the transmission of social inequality across generations, as advantaged families pass on economic, cultural, and social capital that keeps their children in advantaged positions.

distractor_notes.B: Cultural diffusion is the spread of cultural traits between groups, not the intergenerational transmission of advantage.
distractor_notes.D: Structural mobility is broad movement driven by economic change, not the perpetuation of family advantage.
distractor_notes.A: Absolute poverty is a threshold of deprivation, not a mechanism of inherited advantage.

questions.ground_truth_ref: social reproduction (QBook Ch6) - original framing

passages.title: Demographic transition in an industrializing nation
passages.passage_text: Demographers track a nation as it industrializes over two centuries. In the earliest period, both births and deaths are high and the population is roughly stable. Later, sanitation and medicine reduce deaths sharply while births remain high, and the population grows rapidly. Eventually births decline as well, and growth slows. The team also monitors migration into and out of the country.

questions.stem: In the demographic transition model, the stage in which death rates fall sharply while birth rates remain high, producing rapid population growth, is:
questions.explanation: In Stage 2 of the demographic transition, improvements in sanitation, food supply, and medicine lower mortality while fertility stays high, causing rapid population growth.

distractor_notes.A: Stage 1 has both high birth and death rates and roughly stable population.
distractor_notes.C: Stage 4 has both low birth and death rates with slow or stable growth.
distractor_notes.B: 'Demographic stagnation' is not a defined stage; the described gap between falling deaths and high births is Stage 2.

questions.ground_truth_ref: demographic transition Stage 2 (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: In the earliest period described, both birth and death rates are high and population is roughly stable. This corresponds to:
questions.explanation: Stage 1, the pre-industrial stage, is characterized by high birth rates offset by high death rates, so overall population size remains relatively stable.

distractor_notes.B: Stage 3 has falling birth rates as death rates remain low; growth slows but births are declining.
distractor_notes.C: Stage 2 has high births but falling deaths, producing rapid growth, not stability.
distractor_notes.D: Stage 4 has both low births and low deaths, again roughly stable but at low rates, not high ones.

questions.ground_truth_ref: demographic transition Stage 1 (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: Several industrialized nations now have a fertility rate below the level needed to replace the population. The most direct long-term implication is:

options.B: Population decline and an aging population, absent net immigration
options.D: An immediate rise in the birth rate

questions.explanation: When fertility falls below replacement level, the population tends to shrink and grow older over time unless offset by net immigration, because too few children are born to replace the aging cohorts.

distractor_notes.A: Below-replacement fertility slows and reverses growth; it does not cause rapid growth.
distractor_notes.C: Stage 1 features high birth and death rates; below-replacement fertility is a late-stage phenomenon, not a return to Stage 1.
distractor_notes.D: Low fertility does not by itself trigger an immediate rise in births.

questions.ground_truth_ref: below-replacement fertility implications (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: A large number of people permanently leave the country to settle abroad. From the origin country's perspective, this movement is termed:
questions.explanation: Emigration is the movement of people out of a country to settle elsewhere. Immigration is the reciprocal inflow into a destination country.

distractor_notes.A: Immigration is movement into a country, the opposite of what is described.
distractor_notes.B: Natural increase is births minus deaths, not migration.
distractor_notes.D: Internal migration is movement within a country's borders, not across them.

questions.ground_truth_ref: emigration vs immigration (QBook Ch6) - original framing

passages.title: Explaining deviance and social control
passages.passage_text: A criminologist reviews competing sociological explanations of rule-breaking. One account emphasizes the gap between culturally endorsed goals (such as wealth) and the legitimate means available to reach them. Another emphasizes how being publicly identified and treated as a deviant can shape a person's self-concept and future behavior. The researcher also distinguishes formal from informal mechanisms that societies use to enforce norms.

questions.stem: The explanation emphasizing the gap between culturally endorsed goals and the legitimate means to achieve them is:
questions.explanation: Merton's strain theory holds that deviance arises when society emphasizes goals (e.g., wealth) but limits legitimate means to attain them, producing strain that some resolve through deviant adaptations.

distractor_notes.A: Labeling theory focuses on the effects of being labeled deviant, not the goals-means gap.
distractor_notes.B: Differential association attributes deviance to learning it through close associations, not structural strain.
distractor_notes.C: Conflict theory emphasizes power and inequality broadly, not specifically the goals-means discrepancy Merton described.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Merton strain theory (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: The account emphasizing that being publicly identified and treated as a deviant can shape one's self-concept and future behavior is:
questions.explanation: Labeling theory argues that deviance is partly a product of social reaction: once a person is labeled deviant, the label can be internalized and reinforce further deviant behavior.

distractor_notes.B: Strain theory locates deviance in the goals-means gap, not in social labeling.
distractor_notes.C: Structural functionalism examines how deviance serves social functions, not the self-fulfilling effect of labels.
distractor_notes.D: Rational choice theory frames deviance as a cost-benefit calculation, not a labeling process.

questions.ground_truth_ref: labeling theory (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: Being arrested and sentenced by a court for breaking a written law is an example of:
questions.explanation: Formal social control uses codified laws enforced by official institutions (police, courts) that apply formal sanctions such as arrest and sentencing.

distractor_notes.A: Informal social control uses unofficial sanctions like disapproval or ridicule, not legal proceedings.
distractor_notes.C: Anomie is a state of normlessness, not a mechanism of enforcement.
distractor_notes.D: Internalized control is self-regulation through conscience, not external legal action.

questions.ground_truth_ref: formal vs informal social control (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: A person who feels a breakdown of social norms and a sense of purposelessness during a period of rapid social upheaval is best described as experiencing:
questions.explanation: Anomie, a concept from Durkheim, denotes a condition of normlessness in which social norms weaken or conflict, often during rapid change, leaving individuals without clear guidance.

distractor_notes.A: Assimilation is the process of absorbing into a dominant culture, not normlessness.
distractor_notes.B: Stigma is a discrediting mark on identity, not a breakdown of norms.
distractor_notes.D: Resocialization is learning new norms to replace old ones, not the absence of norms.

questions.ground_truth_ref: anomie (QBook Ch6) - original framing

passages.title: Health, medicine, and sociological paradigms
passages.passage_text: Public health researchers study how illness is distributed and understood in society. They document that disadvantaged groups suffer worse health outcomes even at the same hospitals, examine the social expectations placed on people who are ill, and trace how conditions once viewed as personal or moral problems come to be treated as medical conditions. They interpret these observations through several major sociological paradigms.

questions.stem: The finding that disadvantaged groups experience systematically worse health outcomes than advantaged groups is best labeled:
questions.explanation: Health disparities are systematic, avoidable differences in health outcomes across social groups defined by factors such as socioeconomic status, race, or geography.

distractor_notes.A: The sick role describes social expectations of ill individuals, not group differences in outcomes.
distractor_notes.C: Medicalization is the redefinition of non-medical problems as medical, not disparities in outcomes.
distractor_notes.B: The placebo effect is an individual response to inert treatment, unrelated to group-level disparities.

questions.ground_truth_ref: health disparities (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: According to Talcott Parsons' concept of the sick role, a legitimately ill person is:

options.A: Exempted from normal role obligations but obligated to seek help and try to get well
options.B: Held fully responsible for causing the illness and expected to continue normal duties
options.C: Permanently released from all social obligations
options.D: Considered deviant and subject to legal sanction

questions.explanation: In Parsons' sick role, the ill person is exempted from normal responsibilities and is not blamed for the illness, but in exchange is obligated to want to get well and to seek and cooperate with competent help.

distractor_notes.B: The sick role holds that the person is not responsible for the illness and is excused from normal duties, contradicting this option.
distractor_notes.C: The exemption is temporary and conditional on trying to recover, not permanent.
distractor_notes.D: The sick role frames illness as a legitimate temporary status, not as criminal deviance.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Parsons sick role (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: The process by which a condition once regarded as a personal or moral problem comes to be defined and treated as a medical illness is called:
questions.explanation: Medicalization is the process through which non-medical problems (e.g., certain behaviors formerly seen as moral failings) become defined and managed as medical conditions requiring diagnosis and treatment.

distractor_notes.A: Stigmatization is the attachment of a discrediting label, not the reclassification of a problem as medical.
distractor_notes.C: Rationalization (Weber) is the spread of efficiency and calculability in social life, not the redefinition of problems as medical.
distractor_notes.D: Socialization is learning the norms of one's society, unrelated to defining conditions as medical.

questions.ground_truth_ref: medicalization (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: Researchers who interpret the worse health outcomes of disadvantaged groups as a product of unequal power and resources between social classes are applying:
questions.explanation: Conflict theory analyzes society in terms of competition over scarce resources and power. Framing health inequities as a consequence of class-based inequalities in resources and power is a conflict-theory interpretation.

distractor_notes.A: Structural functionalism emphasizes how social parts contribute to stability, not power struggles producing inequity.
distractor_notes.B: Exchange theory analyzes cost-benefit reasoning in interactions, not class-based resource conflict.
distractor_notes.D: Symbolic interactionism focuses on meanings and interactions at the micro level, not macro power structures.

questions.ground_truth_ref: conflict theory paradigm (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: A researcher who studies the meanings that patients and doctors attach to specific gestures and words during a medical visit is working within which paradigm?
questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism is a micro-level paradigm focused on how people create and interpret shared meanings through social interaction, such as the meanings exchanged between patient and physician.

distractor_notes.A: Structural functionalism is a macro-level view of how institutions maintain social order, not micro-level meaning-making.
distractor_notes.B: Conflict theory addresses macro-level power and inequality, not the interpretive detail of an encounter.
distractor_notes.C: The demographic transition model describes population change, not the interpretation of interactions.

questions.ground_truth_ref: symbolic interactionism (QBook Ch6) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A therapist encourages a client to speak whatever comes to mind without censoring, aiming to access unconscious material. This technique, associated with psychoanalysis, is called:
discrete_questions.explanation: Free association is a psychoanalytic technique in which the client verbalizes thoughts freely and uncensored, on the assumption that this reveals unconscious conflicts.

distractor_notes.B: Systematic desensitization is a behavioral therapy for phobias using graded exposure with relaxation.
distractor_notes.C: A token economy is an operant technique reinforcing behavior with exchangeable tokens.
distractor_notes.D: Flooding is intense, prolonged exposure to a feared stimulus, a behavioral technique, not a route to the unconscious.

discrete_questions.stem: A person with a personality disorder marked by disregard for others' rights, deceitfulness, and lack of remorse is most consistent with:
discrete_questions.explanation: Antisocial personality disorder is defined by a pervasive pattern of disregard for and violation of the rights of others, including deceit, impulsivity, and lack of remorse.

distractor_notes.A: Avoidant personality disorder involves social inhibition and fear of rejection, not exploitation of others.
distractor_notes.C: Dependent personality disorder features excessive need to be cared for and submissiveness.
distractor_notes.D: Histrionic personality disorder involves excessive emotionality and attention-seeking, not remorseless rights violations.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: antisocial personality disorder (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: When symptoms are experienced by the individual as consistent with their self-image and cause them no distress, the symptoms are described as:
discrete_questions.explanation: Ego-syntonic symptoms are experienced as consistent with one's self-concept and are not distressing to the person (as in many personality disorders). Ego-dystonic symptoms conflict with the self-image and cause distress.

distractor_notes.A: Ego-dystonic is the opposite: symptoms feel alien and distressing, as in OCD.
distractor_notes.B: Somatic refers to bodily symptoms, not the relationship between symptoms and self-concept.
distractor_notes.D: Prodromal refers to early warning signs preceding a full disorder, not the fit with self-image.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: ego-syntonic vs ego-dystonic (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: In Piaget's theory, incorporating a new experience into an existing mental framework without changing that framework is called:
discrete_questions.explanation: Assimilation is interpreting new information in terms of an existing schema. Accommodation, by contrast, is modifying the schema to fit new information.

distractor_notes.A: Accommodation changes the existing schema to fit new information, the opposite of what is described.
distractor_notes.B: Conservation is understanding that quantity persists despite changes in appearance, not a schema process.
distractor_notes.C: 'Equilibration reversal' is not a Piagetian term.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: assimilation vs accommodation (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A student who studies primarily because she finds the subject fascinating, rather than for grades or rewards, is displaying:
discrete_questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation is engaging in an activity for its inherent satisfaction or interest, not for external rewards. Studying out of genuine fascination is intrinsic.

distractor_notes.B: Extrinsic motivation is driven by external rewards or pressures such as grades, the opposite here.
distractor_notes.C: The overjustification effect describes intrinsic motivation being undermined by external reward, not the interest itself.
distractor_notes.D: Drive reduction concerns satisfying physiological needs, not intellectual interest.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: In Lazarus's model of stress, deciding whether an event is a threat, harm, or challenge is called:
discrete_questions.explanation: Primary appraisal is the initial evaluation of whether a situation is irrelevant, benign, or stressful (a threat, harm, or challenge). Secondary appraisal then evaluates one's ability to cope.

distractor_notes.A: Secondary appraisal evaluates one's resources and options for coping, occurring after the primary appraisal.
distractor_notes.C: Problem-focused coping is a coping strategy aimed at the stressor, not the appraisal of the event.
distractor_notes.D: The general adaptation syndrome (Selye) describes the body's physiological stress stages, not cognitive appraisal.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: primary vs secondary appraisal (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A coping strategy in which a person directly addresses the source of stress (for example, making a study schedule to handle exam pressure) is best classified as:
discrete_questions.explanation: Problem-focused coping targets the stressor itself with concrete actions to change or manage it, such as planning and organizing to reduce exam pressure.

distractor_notes.A: Emotion-focused coping manages the emotional response rather than the stressor itself.
distractor_notes.B: Reappraisal reframes the emotional meaning of the event, an emotion-focused strategy, not direct action on the stressor.
distractor_notes.D: Avoidance coping involves evading the stressor, not directly addressing it.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: problem- vs emotion-focused coping (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A person who has repeatedly held a positive attitude toward an unfamiliar logo simply because they have seen it many times is exhibiting:
discrete_questions.explanation: The mere exposure effect is the tendency to develop a preference for stimuli merely because they have become familiar through repeated exposure.

distractor_notes.B: Cognitive dissonance is discomfort from conflicting cognitions, not liking from familiarity.
distractor_notes.C: Foot-in-the-door is a compliance technique using a small initial request, not attitude from exposure.
distractor_notes.A: Classical extinction is the weakening of a conditioned response, unrelated to preference from familiarity.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: mere exposure effect (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: After succeeding on an exam, a student credits her own intelligence; after failing a later exam, she blames an unfair test. This pattern illustrates:
discrete_questions.explanation: The self-serving bias is the tendency to attribute one's successes to internal factors (ability) and one's failures to external factors (bad luck or unfair conditions), protecting self-esteem.

distractor_notes.C: The just-world hypothesis is the belief that people get what they deserve, not a pattern of self-attribution.
distractor_notes.D: The halo effect is the tendency for one positive trait to color overall impressions of a person.
distractor_notes.B: The fundamental attribution error concerns judging others' behavior dispositionally, not explaining one's own outcomes.

discrete_questions.stem: A bystander is less likely to help a victim when many other people are present than when alone, partly because responsibility is spread across everyone present. This spreading of responsibility is called:
discrete_questions.explanation: Diffusion of responsibility is a key mechanism of the bystander effect: when others are present, each person feels less personally obligated to act, so the likelihood any one individual helps declines.

distractor_notes.A: Pluralistic ignorance (misreading others' inaction as a signal that no help is needed) also contributes to the bystander effect but is not the spreading of responsibility itself.
distractor_notes.C: Deindividuation is loss of self-awareness in a group, not the reduced sense of personal obligation described.
distractor_notes.D: Social facilitation is improved performance on simple tasks when observed, unrelated to helping.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: diffusion of responsibility / bystander effect (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A therapist using Carl Rogers's client-centered approach offers the client warmth and acceptance regardless of what the client says or does. This attitude is termed:
discrete_questions.explanation: Unconditional positive regard is the humanistic therapist's stance of accepting and valuing the client without conditions, which Rogers held fosters growth and congruence between self and experience.

distractor_notes.B: Systematic reinforcement is a behaviorist technique of rewarding target behaviors, not a humanistic attitude.
distractor_notes.D: Self-efficacy is one's belief in the capacity to succeed, a social-cognitive concept, not the therapist's stance.
distractor_notes.A: Transference is a psychoanalytic phenomenon in which feelings toward others are redirected onto the therapist.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: unconditional positive regard (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A worker who normally sleeps at night is switched to a permanent overnight shift and struggles to sleep during the day, feeling persistently fatigued. This difficulty is best attributed to disruption of the:

options.B: Ultradian rhythm within a single sleep cycle

discrete_questions.explanation: The circadian rhythm is the roughly 24-hour internal cycle governing sleep-wake timing. Shift work that opposes this rhythm desynchronizes the internal clock from the sleep schedule, causing poor sleep and fatigue.

distractor_notes.B: Ultradian rhythms cycle within a night (the ~90-minute sleep stages) and are not the source of day-night shift misalignment.
distractor_notes.C: REM rebound is the increase in REM after REM deprivation, not the cause of shift-work fatigue.
distractor_notes.A: Sleep spindles are stage 2 EEG features; they do not govern the 24-hour timing being disrupted.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: circadian rhythm disruption (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: From a functionalist perspective, providing employment for staff is an unintended, unrecognized consequence of a hospital, whereas treating the sick is its recognized, intended purpose. The employment consequence is a:
discrete_questions.explanation: In Merton's functionalist terms, latent functions are the unintended, often unrecognized consequences of a social structure, while manifest functions are the intended, recognized ones. Providing employment is a latent function of a hospital.

distractor_notes.B: A manifest function is the intended, recognized purpose (treating the sick), not the incidental employment effect.
distractor_notes.C: A dysfunction is a harmful consequence for society; providing jobs is beneficial, not a dysfunction.
distractor_notes.D: A social sanction is a reward or punishment enforcing norms, unrelated to functions of an institution.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: manifest vs latent function (QBook Ch6) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: During the acute stress response, the hypothalamus stimulates the anterior pituitary to release ACTH, which drives the adrenal cortex to secrete a key glucocorticoid. That glucocorticoid is:
discrete_questions.explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis culminates in the adrenal cortex releasing cortisol, the primary glucocorticoid stress hormone, which mobilizes energy and modulates the immune response.

distractor_notes.A: Insulin is secreted by the pancreas to lower blood glucose; it is not an adrenal glucocorticoid.
distractor_notes.C: Melatonin is released by the pineal gland to regulate sleep, not by the adrenal cortex under HPA control.
distractor_notes.D: Thyroxine is a thyroid hormone regulating metabolism, released via the HPT axis, not the HPA stress axis.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: HPA axis / cortisol (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: Twin studies find that the concordance rate for a trait is substantially higher in monozygotic (identical) twins than in dizygotic (fraternal) twins. This pattern most directly supports the conclusion that the trait:

options.A: Is entirely determined by the environment
options.D: Is caused solely by a single dominant gene

discrete_questions.explanation: Because monozygotic twins share nearly all their genes while dizygotic twins share about half, a higher concordance in identical twins indicates that genetic factors contribute meaningfully to the trait (it is heritable).

distractor_notes.A: A stronger identical-twin concordance argues for a genetic contribution, not purely environmental causation.
distractor_notes.D: Elevated concordance shows genetic influence but does not establish a single dominant gene as the sole cause.
distractor_notes.B: Heritability does not mean experience is irrelevant; genes and environment typically interact.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: twin studies / heritability (QBook Ch3) - original framing

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish the positive symptoms of schizophrenia from the negative symptoms, giving one example of each.

rubric.description: Positive symptoms = added/excess experiences (e.g. hallucinations, delusions).
required_concepts: positive symptoms are added/abnormal experiences
example such as hallucinations or delusions
disqualifiers: says positive symptoms are 'good' symptoms
defines positive symptoms as losses/deficits

rubric.description: Negative symptoms = reductions/deficits in normal function (e.g. flat affect, avolition).
required_concepts: negative symptoms are deficits/reductions in normal functioning
example such as flat affect, alogia, or avolition
disqualifiers: says negative symptoms are 'bad' symptoms
defines negative symptoms as added experiences

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Positive symptoms are additions to normal experience—hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech. Negative symptoms are losses of normal function—flat affect, avolition, social withdrawal.

free_response_questions.prompt: Name the three levels of Kohlberg's theory of moral development in order, and state what guides moral reasoning at the first and last levels.

rubric.description: Names preconventional → conventional → postconventional, in order.
required_concepts: preconventional, conventional, postconventional
in that order
disqualifiers: puts the levels out of order
omits a level

rubric.description: Preconventional = consequences to self (reward/punishment); postconventional = abstract principles.
required_concepts: preconventional focuses on rewards/punishments/self-interest
postconventional focuses on abstract ethical principles/justice
disqualifiers: swaps preconventional and postconventional reasoning
says preconventional is based on universal principles

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Kohlberg's levels are preconventional, conventional, then postconventional. Preconventional reasoning is driven by rewards and punishments to the self; postconventional reasoning is guided by abstract ethical principles and justice.

free_response_questions.prompt: In Freud's structural model of personality, describe the roles of the id, ego, and superego and the principle each follows.

rubric.description: Id = instinctual drives, pleasure principle; superego = morals/ideals.
required_concepts: id is instinctual drives following the pleasure principle
superego represents morality/conscience/ideals
disqualifiers: says the id is the moral component
says the superego seeks immediate gratification

rubric.description: Ego = mediates id and superego via the reality principle.
required_concepts: ego mediates between id and superego
ego follows the reality principle
disqualifiers: says the ego is purely instinctual
omits the ego's mediating/reality role

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The id houses instinctual drives and follows the pleasure principle; the superego embodies morals and ideals; the ego mediates between them and reality, operating on the reality principle.

free_response_questions.prompt: Explain cognitive dissonance theory: what produces the discomfort, and describe one way people typically reduce it.

rubric.description: Dissonance arises from a conflict between attitudes/beliefs and behavior (or two cognitions).
required_concepts: dissonance is discomfort from conflicting cognitions
mismatch between attitude/belief and behavior
disqualifiers: says dissonance comes from agreement between beliefs and behavior

rubric.description: People reduce it by changing an attitude, changing behavior, or adding justifying cognitions.
required_concepts: reduce dissonance by changing the attitude, the behavior, or rationalizing/adding a cognition
disqualifiers: says people increase the conflict to feel better
gives no valid reduction strategy

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Cognitive dissonance is the discomfort felt when attitudes and behavior (or two beliefs) conflict. People relieve it by changing their attitude, changing their behavior, or adding a new cognition that justifies the inconsistency.

free_response_questions.prompt: Contrast REM sleep with non-REM (slow-wave) sleep, naming one physiological or experiential feature that distinguishes them.

rubric.description: REM: rapid eye movement, vivid dreaming, high/awake-like brain activity, muscle atonia.
required_concepts: REM has rapid eye movements and vivid dreaming
brain activity resembles wakefulness / paralysis of skeletal muscle
disqualifiers: says REM is dreamless deep sleep
swaps REM and NREM features

rubric.description: NREM (esp. slow-wave): slow delta waves, deep restorative sleep, little/no vivid dreaming.
required_concepts: NREM/slow-wave sleep has slow delta waves and is deep/restorative
little or no vivid dreaming
disqualifiers: says NREM shows fast awake-like waves and vivid dreams

free_response_questions.reference_answer: REM sleep features rapid eye movements, vivid dreams, wake-like EEG activity, and skeletal-muscle paralysis. Non-REM (slow-wave) sleep shows slow delta waves and is deep and restorative with little vivid dreaming.

free_response_questions.prompt: Describe Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome, naming its three stages in order and what happens to the body's resources by the final stage.

rubric.description: Names alarm → resistance → exhaustion, in order.
required_concepts: alarm, resistance, exhaustion
in that order
disqualifiers: puts the stages out of order
omits a stage

rubric.description: By exhaustion, the body's resources are depleted, raising vulnerability to illness.
required_concepts: in exhaustion the body's resources/reserves are depleted
increased susceptibility to illness or damage
disqualifiers: says the body is strongest at exhaustion
says resources are fully restored by the final stage

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The General Adaptation Syndrome proceeds alarm → resistance → exhaustion. In the alarm stage the body mobilizes (fight-or-flight); resistance sustains coping; by exhaustion the body's resources are depleted, increasing vulnerability to illness.

free_response_questions.prompt: Define the fundamental attribution error, then contrast it with the self-serving bias in how each explains behavior.

rubric.description: FAE: over-attributing others' behavior to disposition, under-weighting the situation.
required_concepts: fundamental attribution error over-emphasizes dispositional causes for others
under-weights situational factors
disqualifiers: says the FAE over-emphasizes the situation
applies the FAE to one's own successes

rubric.description: Self-serving bias: crediting one's own successes to disposition, failures to the situation.
required_concepts: self-serving bias attributes one's successes to internal factors and failures to external factors
disqualifiers: reverses the self-serving bias
conflates it exactly with the FAE

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The fundamental attribution error is over-attributing others' behavior to their character while ignoring the situation. The self-serving bias is attributing one's own successes to disposition and one's failures to external circumstances.

free_response_questions.prompt: Two brain structures in the limbic system are the amygdala and the hippocampus. State the primary function most associated with each.

rubric.description: Amygdala: emotion, especially fear/threat processing.
required_concepts: amygdala processes emotion, especially fear/threat
disqualifiers: says the amygdala forms long-term memories (that is hippocampus)
assigns the amygdala a motor role

rubric.description: Hippocampus: formation/consolidation of new long-term (declarative) memories.
required_concepts: hippocampus is critical for forming/consolidating new long-term/declarative memories
disqualifiers: says the hippocampus is the primary fear center
says the hippocampus controls heart rate

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The amygdala processes emotion, especially fear and threat detection. The hippocampus is essential for forming and consolidating new long-term (declarative) memories.

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish a caste system from a class system of social stratification with respect to social mobility.

rubric.description: Caste: closed, ascribed status at birth, little/no mobility.
required_concepts: caste system is closed / status ascribed at birth
little or no social mobility
disqualifiers: says caste systems allow free upward mobility
swaps caste and class

rubric.description: Class: more open, achieved status possible, mobility exists.
required_concepts: class system is relatively open
allows social mobility / achieved status
disqualifiers: says class systems permit no mobility
defines class as inherited and fixed for life

free_response_questions.reference_answer: In a caste system, status is ascribed at birth and the system is closed, allowing essentially no mobility. In a class system, status can be achieved and the system is comparatively open, so social mobility is possible.

free_response_questions.prompt: Describe the demographic transition model's overall trend, contrasting birth and death rates in the early (pre-industrial) versus late (post-industrial) stages.

rubric.description: Early stage: high birth rates AND high death rates (slow/low net growth).
required_concepts: early/pre-industrial stage has high birth and high death rates
little net population growth
disqualifiers: says the early stage has low birth and low death rates

rubric.description: Late stage: low birth AND low death rates (population stabilizes).
required_concepts: late/post-industrial stage has low birth and low death rates
population growth stabilizes/levels off
disqualifiers: says the late stage has high birth and high death rates
reverses the trend

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The demographic transition moves from high birth and high death rates (slow growth) in pre-industrial societies, through a phase of falling death rates and rapid growth, to low birth and low death rates (stable population) in post-industrial societies.

free_response_questions.prompt: Contrast labeling theory with strain theory as explanations of deviance, giving the core cause of deviance each proposes.

rubric.description: Labeling theory: deviance results from society labeling an act/person deviant.
required_concepts: labeling theory: deviance stems from being labeled deviant by society
the label shapes identity/behavior
disqualifiers: says labeling theory locates deviance in blocked goals
swaps the two theories

rubric.description: Strain theory: deviance arises from a gap between cultural goals and legitimate means.
required_concepts: strain theory: deviance arises from a mismatch between socially valued goals and access to legitimate means
disqualifiers: says strain theory is about societal labels
ignores the goals/means gap

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Labeling theory holds that deviance emerges when society labels an act or person as deviant, and that label reshapes behavior. Strain theory holds that deviance arises when people lack legitimate means to reach culturally valued goals.

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish ethnocentrism from cultural relativism in how each evaluates another culture's practices.

rubric.description: Ethnocentrism: judging another culture by one's own culture's standards.
required_concepts: ethnocentrism judges other cultures by one's own standards
sees one's own culture as superior
disqualifiers: defines ethnocentrism as judging a culture on its own terms
swaps the two terms

rubric.description: Cultural relativism: understanding a culture on its own terms/standards.
required_concepts: cultural relativism evaluates a culture by its own standards/context
disqualifiers: defines cultural relativism as ranking cultures by one's own values

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Ethnocentrism evaluates another culture using the standards of one's own culture, often treating one's own as superior. Cultural relativism instead seeks to understand a culture's practices within that culture's own context and standards.

free_response_questions.prompt: A neuron's resting membrane potential is about −70 mV. Explain what maintains this negative resting potential, naming the key ion gradient and the pump involved.

rubric.description: Sodium-potassium pump moves 3 Na+ out for 2 K+ in, aiding the gradient (ATP-driven).
required_concepts: the Na+/K+ pump moves 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in
ATP-driven, contributing to the gradient
disqualifiers: says the pump moves Na+ in and K+ out
says resting potential needs no energy/pump at all

rubric.description: Membrane is more permeable to K+ at rest; K+ leak leaves the inside net negative.
required_concepts: at rest the membrane is most permeable to K+ (K+ leak channels)
K+ efflux leaves the inside negative (~ −70 mV)
disqualifiers: says the resting membrane is most permeable to Na+
says the inside is positive at rest

free_response_questions.reference_answer: At rest, the membrane is most permeable to K+, so K+ leaks out and leaves the inside negative (~ −70 mV). The ATP-driven Na+/K+ pump (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in) maintains the ion gradients that sustain this potential.
